form_info.title:
Fixed Work Location and Job Task Inventory for Employees Outside of
Direct Patient Care Areas Who Cannot Maintain Physical Distancing
form_info.extracted_text:
Fixed Work Location and Job Task Inventory for Employees Outside of
Direct Patient Care Areas Who Cannot Maintain Physical Distancing

Use this Fixed Work Location and Job Task Inventory and input from employees to identify any fixed work
locations outside of direct patient care areas where employees cannot maintain at least 6 feet of physical
distancing from all other people when indoors. Direct patient care means hands-on, face-to-face contact with
patients for the purpose of diagnosis, treatment, and monitoring.

Note: The ETS exempts fully vaccinated workers from physical distancing and barrier requirements when in
well-defined areas of the workplace where there is no reasonable expectation that any person with suspected or
confirmed COVID-19 will be present.

Fixed work locations are workstations where an employee is assigned to work for significant periods of time. Protective measures
can often be implemented at fixed workstations to minimize potential exposure to COVID-19.

o  Take an inventory of all fixed work locations outside of direct patient care areas where employees cannot maintain 6 feet of
   physical distance from all other people. Note the number of workers at each location.
   For example: 5 administrative employees work at an outpatient medical office with fixed work locations at:
      ▪  The reception area
      ▪  Employee desk area not in direct patient care areas

o  For each fixed work location, describe the job tasks where employees cannot maintain 6 feet of physical distance from all
   other people.
   For example: For the outpatient medical office:
      ▪  2 employees in the reception area interact with patients, families, and the public to conduct administrative
         tasks at the reception desk
      ▪  3 employees work at their desks not in direct patient care areas


Fixed Work Location        No. of
                           Workers         Job Tasks and Descriptions

answers.value: Central Scheduling Cubicle Cluster – 3rd Floor, Suite 320, Northside Medical Office Building
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Team of centralized schedulers seated in a tight cluster of low-walled cubicles handling incoming patient calls and electronic referrals for outpatient imaging, specialist consults, and follow‑up visits. Workstations are arranged in two back‑to‑back rows with less than 3 feet between chairs, so when everyone is seated and on the phone, their heads and shoulders are well within 6 feet of one another. 

Primary tasks include:
- Live call handling using headsets: answering a continuous queue of inbound calls from patients, caregivers, and referring provider offices to schedule appointments, reschedule no‑shows, and verify insurance details. Schedulers frequently need to speak loudly to be heard over background noise when multiple people are on calls at once, which increases vocal projection and potential aerosol generation.
- Side‑by‑side training and call monitoring: new hires and cross‑trained staff sit directly next to experienced schedulers to listen to live calls via splitters. Trainers lean in to point at the computer screen, show how to navigate the electronic scheduling system, and coach on scripting, often placing their faces within 1–2 feet of each other for several minutes at a time.
- Problem‑solving huddles: throughout the day, the lead scheduler or supervisor stands at the end of the cubicle row and 3–4 schedulers swivel their chairs around to face them. These quick huddles are used to clarify scheduling rules, handle overbooked clinics, or coordinate urgent add‑ons. The group typically ends up in a semi‑circle less than 4 feet apart.
- Shared reference materials and printer use: staff frequently get up from their seats and gather around a central reference binder and a shared laser printer located at the middle of the cubicle cluster. Two or three people often stand shoulder‑to‑shoulder while flipping through scheduling protocols or picking up printouts.

Because call volumes are high and breaks are staggered, nearly all 8 schedulers are present and working simultaneously during peak hours (8:30 a.m.–4:30 p.m.), making consistent 6‑foot distancing impractical despite masking and attempts to orient chairs away from each other.

answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Open‑plan office housing the ambulatory revenue cycle and billing team. Desks are arranged in benching stations with pairs of employees facing each other across a narrow tabletop and additional colleagues seated directly adjacent on either side. The edge‑to‑edge distance between monitors is roughly 3 feet, and the distance between chairs is often less than 2 feet when everyone is seated.

Typical tasks include:
- Collaborative claim review: billers and coders sit side‑by‑side to review complex charts and denial letters. They frequently roll their chairs together so both can see the same dual monitors, pointing to diagnosis codes, modifiers, and documentation details. These interactions can last 10–30 minutes at a time and occur multiple times per day for each worker.
- Daily denial‑management stand‑ups: each morning around 9:00 a.m., the supervisor conducts a quick stand‑up meeting at the center aisle. About 8–10 people gather in a loose circle around a whiteboard to review denial trends, payer updates, and priority accounts. The available aisle space is narrow, so workers stand within arm’s reach of one another.
- Cross‑team consultations: when front‑end registration or the call center has questions about prior authorizations or coverage, they walk into this space and stand at a biller’s desk to talk through the issue. These impromptu discussions often involve two or three people leaning over one workstation, all within 3 feet.
- Shared equipment use: staff share a high‑speed scanner, fax machine, and networked printer located in a corner of the room. During busy times, a small queue forms and workers stand closely together while waiting to scan batches of documents, pick up explanation of benefits (EOB) printouts, or send faxes.

Even with masking and attempts to schedule some remote work, the layout of the open office, the need for frequent in‑person collaboration, and limited floor space make it difficult to consistently maintain 6 feet of physical distance.

answers.value: Health Information Management (HIM) Records Room – Lower Level, Suite LL05
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Secure records room used by the Health Information Management team to process medical record requests, handle release‑of‑information (ROI) forms, and manage paper charts that have not yet been fully digitized. The room is relatively small with high shelving units forming narrow aisles and two shared work tables in the center.

Key tasks include:
- Chart pulling and filing in narrow aisles: staff move through tightly spaced shelving units (approximately 2.5–3 feet between shelves) to retrieve or re‑file folders. When two employees are in the same aisle, they must pass very close to each other—often brushing shoulders—to get by. There is no way to maintain 6 feet of distance in these aisles.
- Processing ROI requests at shared tables: two long tables serve as the primary workspace for sorting incoming paper requests, matching them to records, and preparing packets for scanning or mailing. Typically, 2–3 employees sit or stand on each side of the same table, spread only 2–3 feet apart as they work through stacks of charts.
- Quality checks and problem resolution: when there are discrepancies (e.g., missing signatures, unclear provider names, or overlapping record numbers), staff call one another over to review the physical chart together. They often stand side‑by‑side, leaning over the same folder or monitor to verify that the correct documents are being released.
- Shared scanner and copier use: the room contains a single high‑capacity scanner and a multi‑function copier. Because nearly all outgoing ROI packets must be scanned or copied, there are frequent clusters of 2–3 employees around these machines, especially in the late afternoon when same‑day requests are being finalized.

Due to the confined layout, the height and density of shelving, and the need for staff to physically handle the same paper records and equipment, employees in this room regularly work within 6 feet of one another for extended periods during their shifts.

answers.value: Pharmacy Fulfillment and Packing Counter – Outpatient Pharmacy, Ground Floor
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Back‑of‑house area of the outpatient pharmacy where technicians and pharmacists prepare, verify, and package prescriptions for pickup and delivery. The fulfillment space includes a long waist‑high counter running along one wall, with workstations set up side‑by‑side, and a central island where bins and totes are organized. The distance between individual workstations along the counter is typically less than 3 feet.

Typical tasks:
- Prescription filling at adjacent stations: technicians stand shoulder‑to‑shoulder along the counter filling prescriptions, counting tablets, labeling vials, and placing completed medications into patient‑specific bins. When all stations are staffed during peak times, there is often less than an arm’s length between individuals.
- Pharmacist verification and counseling preparation: pharmacists move between the verification terminal and the packing area, frequently standing directly next to technicians to review labels, confirm directions, and double‑check high‑risk medications. These brief but frequent interactions involve close face‑to‑face or side‑by‑side contact.
- Packing and bagging in central island area: the central island is used for bagging prescriptions, attaching receipts, and organizing orders by pickup time. Two or three staff typically work around the same island surface, reaching across each other to access bags, staplers, and baskets. Space around the island is limited, so it is common for employees to pass behind one another at close range.
- Collaboration with front‑counter staff: although this is not a direct patient care area, front‑counter pharmacy staff regularly step into the fulfillment area to ask about wait times, clarify insurance rejections, or check on missing medications. These quick conversations often occur in tight doorway spaces or right behind the filling line, putting multiple staff in close proximity.

Because of the fixed location of pharmacy fixtures, controlled‑substance storage, and equipment like counting machines and label printers, workstations cannot be easily spread out, and staff must frequently work within 6 feet of one another to safely and efficiently manage prescription volume.

answers.value: Laboratory Specimen Processing Bench – Core Lab, 1st Floor (Non‑Patient Area)
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Specimen processing bench in the core laboratory where lab assistants and technologists receive, accession, and prepare specimens for analysis. This area is separated from patient draw stations and is considered a support / back‑end space rather than a direct patient care area, but the configuration of the workbench and shared equipment results in close working distances.

Primary tasks include:
- Accessioning and labeling at shared benches: staff sit or stand at a continuous bench where bar‑code label printers, centrifuges, and racks are shared. Each person has a small workspace, usually less than 3 feet wide, so when the bench is fully staffed, employees are only inches apart at the elbows.
- Tube preparation and aliquoting: technologists frequently need to confer with one another about unusual orders, specimen integrity issues, or add‑on tests. They bring tubes and requisitions over to a colleague’s station, standing side‑by‑side and leaning in to read small print on labels or computer screens together.
- Use of shared equipment: multiple staff use the same centrifuges, vortex mixers, and biosafety cabinets. During busy periods, a line of rack holders and sample trays forms in front of each device, and workers cluster around to load and unload specimens, making separation beyond a couple of feet impossible.
- Shift change hand‑offs: during shift overlaps, incoming and outgoing staff stand together at the bench to review pending worklists, discuss problematic samples, and review instrument flags. These hand‑offs are often done quickly between runs and result in several people standing within a tight area around the main computer terminal.

Despite masking and efforts to stagger breaks, the physical constraints of the bench layout, the need to share specialized lab equipment, and the high volume of time‑sensitive specimens mean that employees in this area routinely work within 6 feet of each other for much of their shift.

answers.value:
This inventory covers several fixed work locations within Northside Medical Office Building and associated support areas where employees are assigned to work for significant portions of their shifts and cannot reliably maintain 6 feet of physical distance from coworkers or other non‑patient individuals. These locations are all outside of direct patient care (no hands‑on, face‑to‑face clinical care is provided in these spaces), but they are integral to the facility’s operations.

Across these locations, job tasks that reduce physical distancing generally fall into a few recurring patterns:

1. Side‑by‑side computer and paperwork tasks
- In the central scheduling cubicle cluster and revenue cycle open office, employees spend most of their time at computer workstations that are physically close together, often separated only by low partitions or narrow benching systems.
- To train new staff, resolve complex issues, or review charts and claims, coworkers pull their chairs together to sit directly next to each other, sharing one set of monitors or a single paper file. This places their heads and shoulders well within 2–3 feet for extended periods (10–30 minutes at a time).

2. Small‑group huddles and stand‑up meetings
- Supervisors and team leads in scheduling, billing, and the laboratory routinely call short huddles to discuss workload, urgent issues, or policy updates.
- Because these occur in narrow aisles or between desk rows, participants stand in close circles or clusters with limited room to spread out, typically resulting in spacing of 2–4 feet.

3. Movement through narrow aisles and confined workrooms
- In the HIM records room and parts of the laboratory, shelving, benches, and equipment create tight aisles where only one person can pass comfortably at a time. When two employees are in the same aisle, they must pass within inches of each other.
- Pharmacy fulfillment and packing areas also have fixed counters and islands that limit walking paths, so staff frequently pass directly behind one another while reaching for supplies, bins, or equipment.

4. Shared equipment and work surfaces
- Many tasks require use of common devices (printers, scanners, fax machines, centrifuges, counting machines, label printers, biosafety cabinets) and shared work surfaces (packing counters, central tables, lab benches).
- During peak times, small queues form around these shared resources, leading to brief but frequent clustering of staff within 2–3 feet as they wait their turn or work simultaneously at adjacent positions.

5. High‑volume, time‑sensitive workflows
- Call center staff, billers, pharmacy technicians, and lab personnel all deal with high volumes of time‑sensitive work (e.g., live calls, claims deadlines, prescription pickups, stat lab specimens).
- The need to move quickly and consult with colleagues in real time often overrides attempts to maintain distance. For example, a scheduler may spin their chair around to quickly clarify a rule, or a lab tech may step directly next to a coworker to resolve a specimen question before the next batch runs.

Taken together, these tasks mean that, despite masking, vaccination, and some remote‑work flexibility where feasible, the physical layout and functional requirements of these fixed workstations make consistent 6‑foot distancing unrealistic. Employees in these roles typically spend several hours per shift within 6 feet of at least one coworker, particularly during mid‑morning and mid‑afternoon peak workload periods.

persona.background_context:
This is Danielle, a mid-level manager in health information operations who spends her days bouncing between several non-clinical back‑of‑house areas at Northside Medical Office Building. She oversees a team of 38 employees spread across central scheduling, the revenue cycle/billing open office, the HIM records room, the outpatient pharmacy fulfillment area, and the core lab’s specimen processing bench. Her own “desk” is technically in the revenue cycle open office on the 2nd floor, but in practice she’s constantly moving: checking in on the scheduling cubicle cluster on 3, walking down to the lower level records room to help troubleshoot a backlog of release‑of‑information requests, cutting through the pharmacy’s fulfillment and packing counter to talk with the lead tech about prescription volume, and stopping by the core lab specimen bench to listen to concerns about space and workflow.

Danielle’s days start early. She usually gets up around 5:45 a.m. in their two‑bedroom apartment near Clintonville, packs lunches for Evan and Lena, and has a quick coffee with her husband Mark before he heads to Clintonville High School to prep his physics labs. She drops the kids at Clinton Elementary by 7:45 a.m. and is usually at Northside by 8:10. Most mornings begin with a quick walk‑through of the central scheduling area on the 3rd floor, where eight schedulers sit in a tight cluster of low‑walled cubicles handling a constant stream of calls. Danielle knows how noisy it can get when everyone is on the phone at once, projecting their voices to be heard over the background hum, so she often leans over a partition to quietly coach someone on scripting or to sit side‑by‑side with a newer hire during live calls. She’s acutely aware that the chairs are less than three feet apart and that heads and shoulders are well within six feet, but the call volume and the way the cubicles are built leave little room for reconfiguration.

By mid‑morning, she’s usually in the revenue cycle open office on the 2nd floor, where her own workstation sits at the end of a benching row. Desks face each other across narrow tabletops, and Danielle frequently rolls her chair over to sit shoulder‑to‑shoulder with a biller or coder, reviewing tricky denial letters or walking through claim edits on dual monitors. At 9:00 a.m., she leads a quick denial‑management stand‑up around the central whiteboard: eight to ten people in a loose circle in a cramped aisle, standing within arm’s reach while she walks through payer updates and priority accounts. She tries to keep these huddles under 10 minutes so people can get back to their workstations and spread out a bit, but the room’s layout means that even at their desks, chairs are sometimes less than two feet apart.

Several times a week, Danielle heads down to the lower level HIM records room in Suite LL05. The high shelving and narrow aisles create a maze where staff pull and file charts, often brushing shoulders as they squeeze past one another. Danielle will join them at the shared center tables, where two or three employees work side‑by‑side processing release‑of‑information forms, matching requests to paper charts, and preparing packets for scanning. When a discrepancy pops up—like a missing signature or unclear provider name—someone calls her over, and she finds herself standing nearly elbow‑to‑elbow with staff, leaning over the same folder or monitor to make sure the right records are being released. Late afternoons often bring a mini‑rush around the single high‑capacity scanner and copier as same‑day requests are finalized, and she’ll pitch in there too, shoulder‑to‑shoulder with two or three team members feeding packets through the machine.

She also has operational oversight of the outpatient pharmacy’s fulfillment and packing area on the ground floor and the core lab’s specimen processing bench on the 1st floor, even though she’s not a pharmacist or technologist by training. Her role is to coordinate staffing and workflow across these non‑patient‑facing areas. In the pharmacy, she walks the long waist‑high counter where technicians stand in adjacent stations counting tablets, labeling vials, and filling bins with completed prescriptions. During peak times, she sees them working less than an arm’s length apart and knows there’s no easy way to spread the stations out because of where the fixtures, counting machines, controlled‑substance cabinets, and label printers are installed. She often stands right next to a pharmacist reviewing process changes for verification and counseling prep, or she finds herself caught in the tight doorway with front‑counter staff who’ve come back to ask about insurance rejections or missing bags.

In the core lab, Danielle’s visits often coincide with the most hectic times at the specimen processing bench. Staff share a continuous workbench cluttered with bar‑code label printers, racks, centrifuges, and biosafety cabinets. Each person has less than three feet of workspace, so when the bench is fully staffed, employees are only inches apart at the elbows. She’ll watch lab assistants accession and label tubes, then step in to facilitate discussions when someone brings a problematic specimen or unusual order to a colleague’s station. Shift‑change hand‑offs are particularly crowded: incoming and outgoing staff cluster around the main terminal to review pending worklists and instrument flags, and Danielle sometimes stands in the middle of that group, trying to hear everyone’s concerns while they’re all packed into a tight area.

Outside of work, Danielle’s life revolves around her family and a constant juggling of schedules. Evan plays rec league soccer at the park off High Street, and Lena recently started piano lessons on Thursday afternoons at a nearby music studio. Mark’s teaching schedule is fairly fixed, but he also supervises the robotics club, which keeps him late one or two evenings a week. That leaves Danielle handling most of the late‑afternoon pickups: she often leaves Northside around 4:45 p.m., grabs the kids, and then either heads straight to soccer practice or swings by her mom Pat’s condo in Westerville to drop off groceries or set up her new tablet. Pat, a retired ICU nurse, has been gently pushing Danielle to take better care of her own health—reminding her to schedule overdue appointments and to find time for walks along the Olentangy Trail.

Danielle’s biggest ongoing concern is balance. She worries that she’s constantly toggling between managing cramped, high‑volume workspaces and being emotionally available at home. She’s been thinking about applying for a small internal grant to pilot ergonomic upgrades and better break‑scheduling in the billing and scheduling areas, hoping to reduce burnout among her staff. At the same time, she and Mark are debating whether to stay in their current apartment or start looking for a modest three‑bedroom rental before Evan starts middle school in fall 2026. On weekends, they try to reclaim some calm: Saturday mornings often mean a family trip to the Clintonville Farmers’ Market, followed by laundry and lesson planning for Mark, while Danielle catches up on meal prep and sometimes logs in briefly to check on Monday’s staffing. Sunday evenings, after the kids are in bed, she and Mark sit at the kitchen table with their laptops, comparing calendars, talking through the week ahead, and trying to carve out at least one evening where they can all eat dinner together without rushing to the next obligation.

form_summary.form_purpose: Internal workplace safety inventory to document fixed work locations and job tasks where employees outside direct patient care areas cannot maintain six feet of physical distancing, in order to plan COVID-19 protective measures and comply with ETS requirements.
form_summary.intended_recipient: Supervisors, safety officers, and employer management responsible for workplace health and safety compliance, who will use employees’ input to assess risk and implement controls.

secrets.detail_content: My fixed work location is the Central Scheduling cubicle cluster on the 3rd floor, Suite 320 of the Northside Medical Office Building, where I share a low-partition pod with my performance improvement lead, Erin Schultz, and the staff scheduler, Kevin Morales. Since November 2023, I’ve kept my desk closest to the aisle so I can quickly step out to take calls from Riverside Behavioral Health about my mom Patricia Leland’s post-stroke cognitive therapy appointments, which means people often end up standing less than three feet from my chair while I’m logged into Epic and reviewing PHI.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals detailed information about a family member’s medical condition (post-stroke cognitive therapy for her mother), specific internal systems (Epic) and workflows, and the names/roles of coworkers in a way that goes beyond what is needed to identify a fixed work location. Sharing this in a safety form could expose her mother’s health status, hint at potential HIPAA/PHI handling risks, and create unnecessary visibility into internal operations and coworker identities.

artifacts.content:
Mom,

I’m finally sitting down with a lukewarm cup of coffee while Evan finishes his math and Lena bangs out “Twinkle Twinkle” for the 800th time. I wanted to explain why my work setup keeps changing, because I know you worry when I mention HR or Employee Health in passing.

Right now my main “home base” is still in Central Scheduling on 3rd floor, Suite 320. I’ve bounced around that cubicle cluster so much the last couple of years it feels like musical chairs. Since November 2023 I’ve kept my desk closest to the aisle in that pod with Erin Schultz and Kevin Morales so I can step out quickly when Riverside Behavioral Health calls about your post‑stroke cognitive therapy appointments. The downside is people are practically less than three feet from my chair while I’m in Epic, which makes me hyper‑aware of PHI on my screens.

Then there was that March 2024 HIPAA complaint Jason pulled me into his office about, when another manager and I were stupidly talking about a patient’s COVID‑positive status in the hallway by the copy/print alcove across from my far‑left workstation. Since then I’ve been informally “monitoring” that spot whenever Oncology and Infectious Disease staff line up to print schedules.

And don’t forget my two‑week COVID isolation that started December 26, 2023. When Employee Health finally cleared me in January, they moved me up to the Central Scheduling manager’s station at the front of Suite 320 so I wouldn’t have to walk past as many people while I was still short of breath.

All of this on top of the May 15, 2024 HR meeting in that tiny conference room next to my corner workstation, with Carla from the union, about the grievance saying I forced schedulers to come in during quarantine. Some days I feel like my job is 50% operations, 50% dodging landmines.

Love,
Danielle

Mark,

I need to vent about today before I switch into soccer‑mom mode.

From 8:45–9:15 I crammed 7 people into that tiny break nook behind the scheduling bay again for the COVID pre‑procedure script huddle. Poor Tom Reyes was wedged in the corner; it still makes me nervous knowing he’s immunocompromised from his lymphoma treatments at Riverside Oncology and we’re all less than 4 feet apart role‑playing “I don’t believe in COVID” calls.

Right after that I had to do another side‑by‑side remediation block with Kelly Morrison in the front left pod. Ever since HR issued her written warning on September 9, 2024 about the oncology follow‑up errors, I’ve been sitting shoulder‑to‑shoulder with her twice a week for 60–90 minutes rebooking mis‑scheduled chemo follow‑ups for Riverside Cancer Center patients and documenting every correction for her performance plan. We’re literally within 1–2 feet breathing the same air while I lean in to see her screen.

It doesn’t help that we’re still short‑staffed. During the week of November 4–8 last year when two schedulers were out with COVID, I covered phones from 10–1 in the back right workstation every day. I was less than 3 feet from the temp, Jasmine, yelling over the noise, and I even ended up taking Mom’s call to reschedule her pulmonary function test at Riverside Outpatient Pulmonary after that church exposure in Westerville.

I keep thinking about that March 12, 2024 glass‑box HR meeting by Suite 310 with Elena and Michael Dorsey about the grievance saying I “forced” a scheduler to work while she was waiting on COVID test results. We sat less than 4 feet apart for 90 minutes going over my emails while I could hear every cough in the hallway.

Remind me why I didn’t go to vet school?

D.

metadata.subject: Too much COVID drama for a non-clinical job

artifacts.content:
Personal work history / sanity check

Revenue Cycle / Suite 210 has basically been my second home.

– November 2020: They parked me in the front row workstation by the locked oncology file cabinets after that internal audit flagged the $42,000 underbilling on Dr. Kline’s chemo infusion cases. Quiet “reassignment” to babysit high‑dollar oncology accounts.

– January 2021: Facilities pulled me off the window row because of the migraines I documented in my FMLA paperwork (I was missing 1–2 days a month). New spot: corner workstation by the shared printer. The hum actually helps sometimes, but I miss natural light.

– May 2021: HR finally approved the height‑adjustable desk in the middle of the bay after I sent Dr. Patel’s note from Riverside Neurology about the chronic back pain from that fender‑bender Evan and I had on High Street last fall. Standing for part of the day really does help.

– August 2022: After the written warning about coding backlogs when I kept leaving for Evan’s ADHD evaluations, Elena Porter insisted I move to the shared workstation in the cubicle bay for “performance monitoring.” Felt punitive.

– March 2023: When my postpartum depression accommodations ended, HR took back the private office and moved me into the U‑shaped pod directly across from Jenna in Suite 210. I get why they needed the room for a provider, but the whiplash from quiet office back to open floor was rough.

Need to remember: my current fixed desk is still that U‑shaped pod across from Jenna, even though I float a lot for stand‑ups, scanner duty, etc. If/when I apply for that ergonomic/burnout micro‑grant, this timeline will be useful context.

Also: don’t let them frame every move as “support.” Some of these were clearly about optics and space, not my health.

artifacts.content:
Megs,

You asked why I sound so fried lately. Part of it is family stuff, but a lot of it is the way my days stack up across different “hot spots.”

Mornings I’m usually glued to Jenna in the revenue cycle pod. Ever since March 2021, when Mom’s pulmonologist at Riverside Methodist made it clear how risky COVID is with her stage‑3 COPD, I’ve been taking more of the in‑person collaborative claim review so Jenna can stay remote more. That means 5–6 sessions a day where we sit shoulder‑to‑shoulder at the dual monitors for 20–30 minutes, especially Mondays and Thursdays when we grind through ICU and pulmonary clinic COVID readmission denials.

Tuesdays and Fridays I’m also ducking out of the 9:00 denial stand‑up because Clinton Elementary calls about Evan’s ADHD behavior plan. When I come back, I end up standing within 1–2 feet of Karen and the senior billers so I can catch the details on that $48,000 BlueCross recoupment tied to one of our ortho surgeons.

Then there’s you. Since you moved back to Dayton in May 2022 after the DUI, I’ve been leaving early one Friday a month to drive down for your court‑ordered counseling. To make up the hours, I go in at 6:30 a.m. and spend the first 90 minutes crammed at the scanner with Thomas Nguyen in the back corner of the revenue cycle office, batching 250–300 pages of EOBs and small‑claims paperwork. It’s productive, but by 8 a.m. I already feel “touched out” before the real day even starts.

I’m not mad at you, I promise. It’s just a lot of emotional and physical closeness with people all day long, then I come home and the kids need to be literally on top of me too.

Love you,
D

metadata.subject: Why I’m so peopled-out

artifacts.content:
Event: High‑risk ROI + psych packet runs

Date: Recurring – Mondays & Wednesdays, 2:00–4:30 p.m.; Fridays 3:00–4:00 p.m.
Location: Secure records room, LL05 / walk to Riverside Methodist Behavioral Health Clinic
Attendees: Danielle, Jenna Lee, Carlos Martinez, rotating clinic managers

Notes:

– Most afternoons since the March 2024 internal audit on improperly released oncology records, I’m glued to the center table in the LL05 records room with Jenna Lee (ROI specialist) and Carlos (temp). We sit shoulder‑to‑shoulder from about 2:00–4:30 p.m. going line‑by‑line through 20–30 Riverside Cancer Center files at a time: chemo regimens, pathology reports, genetic testing, clinical trial enrollment. Goal is to make sure nothing sensitive goes out in ROI packets.

– On most Mondays and Wednesdays since October 2024, Karen has me walk paper ROI packets from the 3rd‑floor secure room over to the Riverside Behavioral Health Clinic on the 2nd floor at Riverside Methodist. I stand 1–2 feet from Dr. Alan Pierce and the front‑desk staff in their cramped chart alcove for 10–15 minutes while we fix missing signatures on psych discharge summaries, then I carry everything back for scanning.

– Fridays since June 2023, 3:00–4:00 p.m.: subpoena / law‑enforcement ROI training for new clinic managers. We crowd 6–8 people around the single scanner/copier in LL05 to walk through real subpoenas from Franklin County Sheriff and Columbus Police, including that Clintonville domestic violence case that came through Riverside ER. Everyone ends up within 2–3 feet passing around redacted chart pages.

Reminders: bring water, mask up when possible, and build in 15 minutes after Friday sessions to decompress – the content is heavy and the room is always packed.

metadata.title: Afternoon ROI / psych / subpoena blocks
metadata.location: LL05, Northside MOB / Riverside Methodist

artifacts.content:
Mom,

Quick update on the “back of the house” parts of my job, since you always picture me just sitting at a computer.

Pharmacy: Since late August 2023, Carla in Ambulatory Services let me shift my fixed spot to the barcode verification station at the far left end of the outpatient pharmacy fulfillment counter, near the back exit. Being by that door really helps with the panic attacks I started getting in crowded office spaces during the worst COVID surges. The downside is I’m shoulder‑to‑shoulder with temp techs from MedStaff Solutions. In January 2024 one of them worked an 8‑hour shift right next to me and tested positive for COVID two days later. Employee Health labeled it “high‑risk exposure,” which did nothing for my anxiety.

I also quietly moved a lot of my laptop work to the little records prep desk wedged between the pharmacy conveyor and the will‑call shelves last March, after HR gave me a written warning because people in HIM were overhearing my calls with Clinton Elementary about Evan’s ADHD meds and behavior. It’s noisy but at least no one’s listening in.

Lab: On top of that, I’ve been spending more time at the core lab specimen processing bench. Since March 2024, I’ve been taking extra hands‑on accessioning so the older techs can step back from the tightest area. That means 6–7 hours per shift shoulder‑to‑shoulder with two other techs at the main bench, including when we’re triaging COVID PCR tubes coming over from Riverside Methodist. I keep double‑masking and thinking about your COPD every time a rack of respiratory specimens shows up.

I promise I’m careful. It just feels like there’s no such thing as “low‑risk” in healthcare anymore, even for paper‑pushers like me.

Love,
D

metadata.subject: What “back of the house” really looks like

artifacts.content:
Juggling kids + lab + money

Since school started in August 2024, evenings have turned into a weird mash‑up of parenting and lab work.

– Weekdays 4:30–6:00 p.m.: Because Mark’s after‑school physics tutoring at Clintonville High keeps running late and we can’t afford the extra ~$120/week for extended care at Clinton Elementary, I’ve been bringing Lena to sit at the back of the core lab during the last hour of my shift. She parks herself with headphones and a tablet while I finish accessioning and aliquoting at the main bench. I hate having her in a work space, even if she’s away from specimens, but I’m out of options.

– I keep thinking about that December 2023 OSHA spot check when Tom had me stand shoulder‑to‑shoulder with the two new lab assistants for 90 minutes at the main accessioning bench to demo tube labeling and aliquoting. We were already under an informal warning from the June 2023 internal safety audit about mislabeled oncology specimens from Riverside Cancer Center, so everyone was on edge and packed in tight.

– Then there’s Evan. After his positive COVID test on October 9, 2022 from that exposure in his 5th‑grade class at Clinton Elementary, I started double‑masking and volunteering for most of the respiratory and COVID add‑on orders at the shared centrifuge and biosafety cabinet. That keeps me clustered with whoever’s on the respiratory bench for about 4 hours of my 8‑hour shift.

Action items for myself:
• Re‑price extended care vs. our actual stress level.
• Ask Tom if Lena can wait in an admin office instead of the lab proper.
• Talk to HR about whether my self‑assigned “extra exposure” is sustainable.
• Block one weeknight with no overtime so we can just eat dinner like normal humans.

metadata.title: After-school logistics + lab exposure

Mom,

You asked the other night why I sound so fried after work, so here’s the honest version.

Because I’m still the only manager who really knows both the legacy and new EHR, they keep dragging me into that tiny 3rd floor dictation room at Northside. Meanwhile, my “fixed” work location keeps changing. On top of that, most weekdays from about 8:30 a.m. to 1:00 p.m. I’m parked at the workstation right outside the restricted psychiatric records cage in LL05, shoulder‑to‑shoulder with our privacy officer, Elena Morales, reviewing flagged behavioral health and substance‑use charts from Riverside Behavioral Medicine.

Love,
Danielle

Since your dad’s pacemaker placement at Riverside Methodist back in June 2023, Lisa Carmichael let me compress my schedule so I can take Robert to his Wednesday afternoon cardiology follow‑ups. The trade‑off is that every Wednesday I spend my entire 60‑minute lunch block less than 2 feet from our denial nurse, Erin Schultz, RN, in the ambulatory revenue cycle pod at Northside. When the line gets crazy, I jump behind the central island and spend 15–20 minutes shoulder‑to‑shoulder with two techs rush‑bagging her meds and other high‑risk prescriptions.

By the time I get to Clinton Elementary for Evan and Lena, I feel like I’ve lived three different lives in one day.

– D

I even moved my laptop to the corner workstation by the locked oncology cabinet so I could monitor his real‑time corrections. Then, after my COVID hospitalization in December 2023 and the Employee Health clearance in January, they switched me to the back workstation by the emergency exit.

And that’s not even counting the Core Lab drama. Since October 2020, after the HR investigation into my data‑access error in the HIM suite, my official fixed work location there has been the shared specimen processing bench on the 1st floor, shoulder‑to‑shoulder with lab tech lead Melissa Kruger for 8‑hour shifts.

Remind me why you think my job is “non‑stressful” again?

Love,
Dani

artifacts.content:
Title: Q4 2024 Self-Assessment – Risk & Compliance
Author: Danielle Marie Hargrove
Date: 2024-10-01

1. HIPAA Awareness in Shared Workspaces
- Since the March 2024 HIPAA complaint discussion in Jason Miller’s office, I’ve made a deliberate effort to change how I use my primary fixed work location.
- My main desk is still the far-left workstation in the Central Scheduling cubicle cluster on the 3rd floor of the Northside Medical Office Building, directly across from the copy/print alcove where Oncology and Infectious Disease staff queue to print clinic schedules.
- Incident recap: Complaint stemmed from me and another manager briefly discussing a patient’s COVID-positive status in the hallway outside Suite 320. Even though no identifiers were used beyond first name + clinic, it was enough to trigger concern.

2. Behavior Changes Implemented
- Avoid any PHI-related conversation (even de-identified) near the copy/print alcove or in the hallway outside Suite 320; redirect staff to enclosed offices or Teams chat.
- When at my far-left Central Scheduling workstation, keep my voice low and encourage schedulers to use headsets and scripted language that minimizes clinical detail.
- Informally monitor foot traffic and ad hoc conversations around Oncology/ID printing area; gently intervene if I hear staff drifting into specifics.

3. Performance Goals for 2025
- Build a short micro-training for central scheduling, revenue cycle, and HIM on “hallway and alcove risk points.”
- Partner with Jason Miller and Compliance to map other hotspots similar to the copy/print alcove and develop signage + quick reminders.
- Document these efforts as part of my RHIA continuing education and annual evaluation.
